question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a TopicMessageFilter with a startTime set to the exact consensusTimestamp of a known message so that the >= comparison in filterMessage() causes that message to be re-delivered as if it were a new event, allowing the attacker to make historical messages appear as current ones and reorganize the perceived transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user open multiple simultaneous subscriptions with overlapping startTime windows on the same topicId, causing filterMessage() to deliver the same TopicMessage objects to multiple streams in different orders, making it appear that the same transaction occurred at different points in the topic history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() only validates topicId equality and consensusTimestamp >= startTime without checking message sequence numbers, allowing out-of-order messages to pass through and be presented to consumers as a valid ordered history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user set startTime to a value equal to an existing consensusTimestamp in the topic, causing filterMessage() to include that message in a new subscription stream, effectively replaying a historical transaction as if it were newly confirmed and disrupting the perceived ordering of the ledger?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of sequence number gap detection in filterMessage() to receive a stream where messages with non-consecutive sequence numbers are silently accepted, making it impossible for downstream consumers to detect that the transaction history has gaps or has been reorganized?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly subscribe and unsubscribe to the same topic with different startTime values to cause the underlying listener to emit messages in a non-chronological order across multiple Flux streams, allowing an observer to reconstruct a false transaction history from the combined output?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() applies filterMessage() after the underlying listener emits, meaning that if the underlying listener emits messages out of order, no reordering or rejection occurs in listen(), allowing a reorganized message sequence to reach the subscriber?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger listen() with a startTime just before a known high-volume burst of messages, causing the Flux to emit those messages in whatever order the underlying listener provides them, with no enforcement of ascending consensusTimestamp ordering, resulting in a reorganized view of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of per-subscriber deduplication in listen() to receive the same TopicMessage multiple times across reconnections with the same startTime, making it appear that a transaction was confirmed more than once in the topic history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user open a subscription with startTime set to 0 (the minimum allowed by @Min(0) in TopicMessageFilter) to receive the entire topic history from the beginning, and by combining multiple such subscriptions with different endTime values, reconstruct a selectively filtered view of history that omits certain transactions, creating a false narrative of the topic's transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTopicListener()] Can an unprivileged external user, by observing differences in message ordering between POLL, REDIS, and SHARED_POLL listener types (selected via getTopicListener()), infer which listener type is active and then craft timing-based subscriptions that exploit the specific ordering guarantees (or lack thereof) of that listener type to receive messages in a reorganized sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getTopicListener()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the default branch of the switch in getTopicListener() by causing a configuration race condition, resulting in an UnsupportedOperationException that leaks the internal listener type name, which the attacker can use to tailor a history-reorganization attack specific to the active listener implementation?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user craft or replay a TopicMessage with a consensusTimestamp set far in the future (large nanosecond value) so that the division by 1000000 in recordMetric() produces a millisecond value larger than System.currentTimeMillis(), causing the latency check (latency > 0) to suppress metric recording and hide evidence of message reordering from monitoring systems?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the integer overflow risk in recordMetric() where consensusTimestamp (a long in nanoseconds) divided by 1000000 could produce unexpected values for very large timestamps, causing the latency metric to be recorded incorrectly and masking the true ordering of messages in monitoring dashboards?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user submit messages with consensusTimestamp values that, after division by 1000000, equal System.currentTimeMillis(), causing the latency > 0 check in recordMetric() to always be false and preventing any latency metrics from being recorded, effectively blinding operators to message delivery anomalies that could indicate history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() uses message.getTopicId().equals(filter.getTopicId()) without verifying the message's internal sequence number continuity, allowing a scenario where messages from different topic shards or partitions with the same topicId but different sequence numbers are interleaved, reorganizing the apparent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe with a startTime that is exactly 1 nanosecond before a known consensusTimestamp, ensuring that filterMessage() includes that message, and then use the same subscription to also receive newer messages, creating a stream where an old transaction appears to precede newer ones in a way that misrepresents the actual consensus ordering?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of monotonically increasing consensusTimestamp enforcement in the Flux pipeline of listen() to receive a stream where a late-arriving message (with a lower consensusTimestamp than a previously delivered message) is included without rejection, causing the subscriber to see a reorganized transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not enforce a maximum number of concurrent subscriptions per topicId, allowing the attacker to open thousands of subscriptions with slightly different startTime values to create a distributed view of topic history that, when aggregated, presents a reorganized sequence of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the dual-condition filter in filterMessage() (topicId equality AND consensusTimestamp >= startTime) by providing a startTime of 0 to receive all historical messages for a topic, then selectively presenting a subset of those messages to third parties as the complete history, effectively reorganizing the visible transaction record?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the reactive Flux pipeline in listen() where doOnNext(this::recordMetric) is called after filter(), meaning that if filterMessage() passes a message with an anomalous consensusTimestamp, the metric is recorded without any integrity check, allowing the attacker to pollute the metrics system with false latency data that obscures history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of upper-bound validation on consensusTimestamp in filterMessage() to receive messages with future-dated timestamps that have not yet been confirmed by consensus, causing those messages to appear in the history stream before they are actually finalized, reorganizing the apparent order of pending and confirmed transactions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() returns Flux.empty() when listenerProperties.isEnabled() is false without signaling an error, causing subscribers to silently receive no messages and incorrectly conclude that no transactions have occurred in the topic, effectively presenting an empty transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate the runningHash or runningHashVersion fields of TopicMessage, allowing messages with incorrect or manipulated hash chains to pass through the filter and be presented to subscribers as valid history, breaking the cryptographic integrity of the transaction sequence?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: filterMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of sequenceNumber validation in filterMessage() to receive a stream where messages with duplicate sequence numbers (same sequence number, different content) are both delivered, making it impossible for the subscriber to determine which represents the true transaction history?
